figures.caption: Figure 17: User method preferences. Shown are the preference counts of methods in editing tasks in which the method was chosen by the majority (at least 3 of 4 participants). We show results perparameter and style variant (“no_style" is the photography style).

Figure 25: Influence of increasing stylization strength - extension of Fig. 15. For our approach, the parameter (λA) is incrementally adjusted, while text-based methods use classifier-free guidance scale and the editing prompts as defined in Sec. B. Comparisons are made against InstructDiffusion [GYH∗24], InstructPix2Pix [BHE23], and StyleBooth [HMJ∗24].

Figure 11: Comparison of continuous editing. Top row: our method varies contour width smoothly by adjusting parameter values and activation (t) time steps, demonstrating consistent control over line thickness. Bottom row: Using IP2P [BHE23] with same input, we attempt to replicate an increase in contour width using editing prompts or varying CFG. In (h) and (i) we apply the editing prompt P=“make it have thick strokes" and text cfg of 12/13, prepending a “very" in (j). In (k) we use Poutlines = “Add outlines”, and in (l) Poutlines = “thick outlines”.
Figure 24: Qualitative comparison to related methods. We show results from the user study. Note that for fair comparison, all images were generated at the same parameter or cfg setting.

Figure 3: Overview of our approach. During training, attention layers (dark orange) of an edge-conditioned Controlnet are finetuned to reproduce the images of a synthetic dataset with varied effect settings λA. During inference, the finetuned network acts as a stylistic control to a pretrained Controlnet and LDM (SD), modifying the attribute A in the image using guidance gA while adhering to content and style.

Figure 22: Parameter variation for the Artbench-trained model. The parameter encodes the “expressionism" style, and we use the prompt “a pastel drawing of a anime style girl" to generate the original. Decreasing the parameter increases the realism of the image since parameter setting 1 encodes an artwork, and 0 a photo.

Figure 23: Training progress with different regularizations. Note that the outputs are at λdepth power = 1, thus at the maximum of the learned range and a strong stylization without significant content alterations from the original image are therefore desireable.

Figure 21: Extended Fig. 8. Outputs of only the Controlnet (gA) for different parameters, conditioned on the edge map (a). All parameters are set to strength λ = 1, generating using prompt “a photo of a woman".
Figure 19: Similarity of parameters over training steps

Figure 18: Training behaviour for parameters and regularization strength. We measure the LPIPS between images generated with λA = 1 and the original Controlnet outputs IR filtered using an algorithmic filter, with the same parameter setting. Results are averaged over our testing dataset. Regularization and more steps generally lead to an increase in stylization strength.
Figure 20: Screenshot from user the study

Figure 9: Parameter encoding ablation. We measure LPIPS of outputs to the unedited original as λA (depth power of the watercolor filter) increases. Using an extra attention layer (our method) provides a stable response across the parameter range.
Figure 26: Extended Fig. 12, varying the activation time step and the parameter (λ). Generation prompt: “a pastel drawing of a girl with a pearl earring".
